questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of white, lacy patches on the inside of both cheeks. She is otherwise well, non-smoker, and takes no regular medications. She has no skin lesions elsewhere. On examination, there are bilateral, asymptomatic, reticular white striae on the buccal mucosa that cannot be scraped off. Single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic reticular white striae (Wickham striae) on the buccal mucosa, bilateral and asymptomatic, are pathognomonic for oral lichen planus. Oral candidiasis scrapes off; lichenoid reaction is usually unilateral and related to a drug; leukoplakia is a white patch that cannot be scraped but is more often solitary and premalignant; pemphigus presents with painful erosions and blisters.","difficulty":"easy

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man presents with intensely itchy, purple, flat-topped papules on his wrists and lower back. The lesions have been present for 3 weeks. He is otherwise healthy. On examination, there are polygonal papules with fine white lines (Wickham striae) on the surface. Single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of intensely pruritic, purple, polygonal, planar papules with Wickham striae is diagnostic of lichen planus. Psoriasis has silvery scale; eczema is erythematous with ill-defined borders; lichen simplex chronicus results from chronic scratching with lichenification; pityriasis rosea has a herald patch and collarette scaling.","difficulty":"easy

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with thinning and ridging of several fingernails over 6 months. She has also noticed a 'wing-like' appearance of the cuticle on one nail. She has no other skin or oral lesions. On examination, there is longitudinal ridging, thinning, and pterygium (dorsal pterygium) affecting the thumb and index finger nails. Single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Pterygium (proximal nail fold adhesion to the nail bed) is a hallmark of nail lichen planus, along with thinning and ridging. Onychomycosis causes thickening and discoloration; trauma gives irregular damage; psoriasis causes pitting, oil spots, and onycholysis; alopecia areata gives pitting but not pterygium.","difficulty":"medium

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of hypertension (on amlodipine) presents with itchy, thickened, reddish-purple plaques on both shins. He has had them for over a year and they have not responded to emollients. On examination, there are well-defined, hyperkeratotic, violaceous plaques with a smooth surface. No oral lesions. Single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hypertrophic lichen planus presents as thick, pruritic, violaceous plaques on the lower legs, often chronic and resistant to treatment. Lichen simplex chronicus is from rubbing and has lichenification; nodular prurigo has discrete nodules; psoriasis scale is silvery; stasis dermatitis has hemosiderin deposition and oedema.","difficulty":"medium

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman of South Asian origin presents with progressive, asymptomatic, dark brown patches on her face and neck over several months. She has no history of sun exposure or drug intake. On examination, there are ill-defined, slate-grey macules on the sun-exposed areas of the face, especially the temples and neck. No preceding inflammation. Single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Lichen planus pigmentosus presents with slate-grey macules on sun-exposed areas in darker skin types, without preceding inflammation. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation follows an inflammatory rash; melasma is brown and centrofacial; fixed drug eruption is single or few round lesions; ashy dermatosis is more widespread and often on trunk.","difficulty":"hard

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with itchy purple papules on her flexor wrists and lower back. She has no oral lesions or nail changes. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Histopathology shows saw-tooth rete ridges, hypergranulosis, and a lichenoid infiltrate, which is diagnostic of lichen planus. Direct immunofluorescence is used for autoimmune blistering disorders, not typical LP. Patch testing diagnoses allergic contact dermatitis, ANA for lupus, and dermoscopy is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man has asymptomatic white lacy streaks on both buccal mucosae. He is concerned about oral cancer. What investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis and exclude dysplasia?
options: Incisional biopsy of lesional tissue for histopathology
questions.explanation: Incisional biopsy provides definitive histology (lichenoid interface dermatitis) and rules out dysplasia or carcinoma. Exfoliative cytology is insufficient for diagnosis. Hepatitis C serology is associated but not diagnostic. CT scan and salivary gland biopsy are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman has progressive thinning and ridging of several fingernails with pterygium formation on one nail. She has no skin or oral lesions. What is the most useful investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Skin biopsy from the nail fold
questions.explanation: Nail matrix biopsy reveals lichenoid infiltrate and hypergranulosis, confirming nail lichen planus. Fungal culture is for onychomycosis, which does not cause pterygium. Dermoscopy is non-diagnostic, ANA screens for autoimmune disease, and nail fold biopsy misses the matrix.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has thick, violaceous, pruritic plaques on his shins that have been present for months. A biopsy shows irregular epidermal hyperplasia, hypergranulosis, and a lichenoid infiltrate. What histological feature best distinguishes hypertrophic lichen planus from lichen simplex chronicus?
questions.explanation: Saw-tooth rete ridges are characteristic of lichen planus, whereas lichen simplex chronicus shows regular acanthosis without saw-toothing. Hypergranulosis can be seen in both. Colloid bodies are more common in LP but not specific. Dermal fibrosis is typical of LSC, and plasma cells are not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has painful erosive lesions on her vulva and also has oral lesions. She has not improved with first-line topical steroids. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis and rule out vulval intraepithelial neoplasia?
questions.explanation: Vulval biopsy is essential to show lichenoid infiltrate and exclude VIN. Direct immunofluorescence is used for pemphigus/pemphigoid, HPV testing for warts, syphilis serology for chancre, and patch testing for contact dermatitis, none of which are the primary differential here.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of itchy, flat-topped, violaceous papules on her wrists and ankles. She has no mucosal involvement and is otherwise well. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical potent corticosteroid (e.g., clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream)
questions.explanation: Mild-to-moderate cutaneous lichen planus is first-line treated with potent topical corticosteroids. Systemic therapies are reserved for severe or refractory disease.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a 2-week history of rapid-onset, widespread violaceous papules covering >50% of his body surface area, with severe pruritus, presents to dermatology. What is the first-line treatment for this severe presentation?
options: Topical potent corticosteroid (e.g., clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream)
questions.explanation: For severe, acute, widespread cutaneous lichen planus, oral corticosteroids are first-line to rapidly control inflammation. Phototherapy and other systemic agents are second-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has painful, erosive oral lichen planus on the buccal mucosa and tongue, confirmed by biopsy. First-line topical treatment has failed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next-line systemic treatment?
options: Oral mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily
questions.explanation: For erosive oral lichen planus refractory to potent topical steroids, short courses of oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone) are first-line systemic therapy. Other immunosuppressants are second-line.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with hypertrophic lichen planus on his shins, unresponsive to potent topical corticosteroids under occlusion, is considered for intralesional therapy. Which of the following is the most appropriate intralesional treatment?
questions.explanation: Intralesional corticosteroids (e.g., triamcinolone acetonide) are the standard second-line treatment for hypertrophic lichen planus when topical therapy fails. The other options are not used for this condition.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with chronic hepatitis C (not on treatment) develops lichen planus on her trunk and extremities. She was recently started on a new medication by her hepatologist. Which of the following drugs should be avoided as it can exacerbate lichen planus?
questions.explanation: Interferon-alfa is known to trigger or exacerbate lichen planus. In patients with hepatitis C and active lichen planus, interferon should be avoided; direct-acting antivirals are safer.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of itchy, flat-topped, violaceous papules on her flexural wrists and lower back. Some lesions have fine white reticulations. She is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure and advise no treatment is needed
Prescribe a potent topical corticosteroid (e.g., clobetasol propionate 0.05%)
Perform a skin biopsy for histology
Prescribe oral prednisolone 30 mg daily for 2 weeks
questions.explanation: Classic lichen planus can be diagnosed clinically; first-line treatment is a potent topical corticosteroid to reduce pruritus and clear lesions. Biopsy is unnecessary unless the diagnosis is uncertain. Reassurance alone is inadequate for symptomatic disease.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known hypertension presents with painless white lacy patches on his buccal mucosa bilaterally. He finds spicy foods uncomfortable. There are no erosions or ulcers. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 1 week
Prescribe oral metronidazole 400 mg three times daily for 7 days
Advise avoidance of spicy foods and reassurance
Prescribe betamethasone mouthwash (500 μg in 10 mL water, used as a mouthwash)
questions.explanation: Non-erosive oral lichen planus is managed with topical corticosteroids, such as betamethasone mouthwash. Systemic steroids are reserved for severe erosive disease. Avoidance of triggers may help but is not the most appropriate next step alone.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man on lisinopril, simvastatin, and regular ibuprofen develops a widespread, intensely pruritic eruption of purple papules that coalesce into plaques with bullae on his lower legs over 2 weeks. He is systemically well. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Perform a punch biopsy of a bullous lesion
questions.explanation: Drug-induced lichen planus is commonly caused by NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, and statins. The immediate priority is to discontinue the most likely culprit (ibuprofen), as this may lead to resolution. Stopping all drugs may be unnecessary and could cause harm. Biopsy and topical steroids are secondary.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with a 6-month history of cutaneous lichen planus now presents with painful vulval erosions and a patch of scarring alopecia on the scalp. She is systemically well. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform a scalp biopsy to confirm diagnosis
Prescribe a high-potency topical corticosteroid for both areas
Refer to dermatology for urgent specialist assessment
questions.explanation: Mucosal and scalp involvement indicates severe or complicated disease requiring specialist management. Urgent dermatology referral is needed to coordinate treatment, consider biopsy for scarred areas, and potentially start systemic therapy. Topical steroids alone are insufficient for this extent.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks gestation presents with itchy, flat-topped violaceous papules on her shins and lower back. She is otherwise healthy. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure her that no treatment is needed
Prescribe a moderate-potency topical corticosteroid (e.g., betamethasone valerate 0.1%)
Prescribe oral prednisolone 20 mg daily for 2 weeks
questions.explanation: Topical corticosteroids are safe in pregnancy when used in limited amounts and for short duration. A moderate-potency steroid is appropriate for symptomatic lichen planus. Emollients alone are insufficient. Systemic steroids are reserved for severe cases. Specialist referral is not immediately needed.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a 3-month history of cutaneous lichen planus on her wrists and shins presents with new-onset dysphagia and weight loss. On examination of the oral cavity, you see lacy white streaks on the buccal mucosa. Which single finding on oral examination would most strongly suggest oesophageal involvement requiring urgent endoscopy?
options: Desquamative lesions on the posterior pharyngeal wall
White reticular patches on the buccal mucosa
questions.explanation: Desquamative lesions on the posterior pharyngeal wall raise suspicion for oesophageal lichen planus, which can cause strictures and requires urgent endoscopy to prevent complications. The other options are typical oral LP findings but do not indicate oesophageal involvement.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with an acute eruption of pruritic, violaceous papules on his trunk and limbs. He recently started taking a new antihypertensive medication. Which accompanying laboratory finding would most strongly suggest a lichenoid drug eruption rather than idiopathic lichen planus?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is a common feature of lichenoid drug eruptions and helps distinguish them from idiopathic lichen planus, where eosinophils are typically absent. The other findings are non-specific or more characteristic of other conditions.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a 5-year history of hypertrophic lichen planus on his lower legs presents with a rapidly enlarging, hyperkeratotic nodule that has become ulcerated. What is the most important diagnosis to exclude by biopsy?
questions.explanation: Hypertrophic lichen planus can undergo malignant transformation, and a rapidly enlarging, ulcerated nodule is a red flag for squamous cell carcinoma. Biopsy is essential to distinguish it from other malignancies or benign mimics.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known lichen planus develops widespread, tense blisters on an erythematous base, accompanied by fever and systemic illness. Which finding on examination would most strongly suggest the life-threatening diagnosis of lichen planus pemphigoides rather than typical bullous lichen planus?
options: Blister roof with neutrophilic infiltrate on biopsy
Tense blisters only on the palms
questions.explanation: Tense blisters on the palms and soles are characteristic of lichen planus pemphigoides, which can be severe and requires prompt systemic immunosuppression. The other options may be seen in both or are non-specific.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with oral lichen planus reports persistent pain and a non-healing ulcer on the lateral border of the tongue for 3 months. Which clinical feature should prompt immediate biopsy to rule out malignancy?
options: White reticular striae surrounding the ulcer
Erythroplakic area adjacent to the ulcer
questions.explanation: Erythroplakia (a red, velvety patch) carries a high risk of dysplasia and malignant transformation, especially when associated with a non-healing ulcer. Biopsy is urgent. The other features are less specific for malignancy.
